coredata.dc:title: One stone, two birds: Gastrodia elata-derived heteroatom-doped carbon materials for efficient oxygen reduction electrocatalyst and as fluorescent decorative materials
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Shuyan
Fan, Hao
Chen, Yanli
Li, Liang
Bando, Yoshio
Golberg, Dmitri
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate a one-stone-two-birds strategy for synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon materials via hydrothermal treatment of Gastrodia elata. The solid product followed by further ionothermal pyrolysis exhibits excellent electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction via a dominant four-electron oxygen reduction pathway in alkaline medium, and shows superior crossover resistance, CO tolerance, and catalytic stability to a commercial Pt/C catalyst. The supernatant exhibits strong blue fluorescence under UV radiation, showing its promising potential as new-generation decorative materials combining with Chinese folk paper-cut art and displaying mysterious and stimulating stage effects. Given these excellent electrocatalytic and optical properties, ease of preparation, and abundant resources, such one-stone-two-birds methodology not only endows the products with desirable functionality, but also timely reminds our researchers of the well-accepted saying that things should be used to their best advantages, which becomes more and more urgent especially in recent days characteristic of progressive resource scarcity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fuel cells
Oxygen reduction reaction
Catalysis
Fluorescent
Carbon

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the N-doped carbon materials
Study on the supernatant product S2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A) of the as-prepared S1-AZ-800 and the magnified one (B) of the selected part in (A). TEM image (C) and HRTEM one (D) of S1-AZ-800. XRD pattern (E) and nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of the S1-AZ-800.

(A) Raman spectrum and (B) FT-IR pattern of S1-AZ-800. (C) Wide XPS survey scan of S1-AZ-800. High-resolution C1s (D) and N1s (E) peaks of S1-AZ-800. (F) Possible structure of an O, N-rich S1-AZ-800 (not drawn to scale).

(A) CV curves of S1-AZ-800, S1-BZ-800, and bared-glassy carbon electrode in N2-saturated and O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. (B) RDE curves for S1-AZ-800 (ca. 0.05mg load) in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH with different speeds. The inset shows the K−L plots derived from the RDE measurements. Scan rate: 10mVs−1. C, D) CV curves of S1-AZ-800 and 20wt% Pt/C (JM) in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution with and without 10vol% methanol at a scan rate of 10mVs−1. (E) The percentage of current density (j) versus time chronoamperometric responses obtained at the 20wt% Pt (JM) and S1-AZ-800 electrodes at −0.3V in O2-saturated 0.1m KOH. The arrow indicates the introduction of N2 or CO into the electrochemical cell. (F) Electrochemical stability of S1-AZ-800 (1) and 20wt% Pt/C (JM) (2) as determined by continuous cyclic voltammetry in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH. Scan rate: 50mVs−1.

(A) UV−vis absorption and (B) PL spectra of S2. The inset in (A) is photographs of S2 under 365nm UV irradiation and daylight, respectively. The photographs of an interesting Chinese traditional picture on commercially available filter paper after being soaked in S2 under 356nm UV light (C) and daylight (D).
Chemical compositions and textural characteristics of S1-BZ-800 and S1-AZ-800.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Specific surface area from multiple BET method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Micropore surface area from the t-plot method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The t-method external surface area (S Mes=S BET–S Mic).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Atomic ratio data from XPS analyses.

head.ce:title: One stone, two birds: Gastrodia elata-derived heteroatom-doped carbon materials for efficient oxygen reduction electrocatalyst and as fluorescent decorative materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, Henan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Thin Films, Soochow University, Suzhou 215006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Thin Films, Soochow University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Center for Materials Nanoarchitectonics (MANA), National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS), Namiki 1-1, Tsukuba, Ibaraki 305-0044, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Center for Materials Nanoarchitectonics (MANA), National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate a one-stone-two-birds strategy for synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon materials via hydrothermal treatment of Gastrodia elata. The solid product followed by further ionothermal pyrolysis exhibits excellent electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction via a dominant four-electron oxygen reduction pathway in alkaline medium, and shows superior crossover resistance, CO tolerance, and catalytic stability to a commercial Pt/C catalyst. The supernatant exhibits strong blue fluorescence under UV radiation, showing its promising potential as new-generation decorative materials combining with Chinese folk paper-cut art and displaying mysterious and stimulating stage effects. Given these excellent electrocatalytic and optical properties, ease of preparation, and abundant resources, such one-stone-two-birds methodology not only endows the products with desirable functionality, but also timely reminds our researchers of the well-accepted saying that things should be used to their best advantages, which becomes more and more urgent especially in recent days characteristic of progressive resource scarcity.
A one-stone-two-birds strategy for synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon materials with excellent electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction and as new-generation decorative materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Doping carbon materials with heteroatoms has gained increasing interest in the past years because it provides an attractive means of effectively tuning their intrinsic properties associated with a wide spectrum of advanced applications in energy conversion and storage [1], light harvesting [2], optoelectronic devices [3], and bioimaging [4]. Besides some examples of boron-doped carbon [5], sulfur-doped carbon [6], and phosphorous-doped carbon [7], nitrogen (N) is an especially widely studied doping candidate owing to the unique electronic properties, which originate from the conjugation between the graphitic π -system and the N lone-pair [8]. To date, different synthetic approaches for the formation of various N-doped carbon materials or composites thereof have been shown [9]. In most cases, complex procedures, purified chemical agents, or expensive hardware are unavoidable. Thus, it is highly desirable but still an immense challenge to produce functional carbon with an efficient strategy from the cheap and renewable resources [10]. On the other hand, to the best of our knowledge, just only one kind of products, either supernatant or solid one, produced during doped carbon materials synthesis has been studied. And this does not solve a severe problem of progressive resource scarcity.

It is known that Gastrodia elata is a saprophytic perennial herb of the Orchidaceae family. It is successfully used in medicine for calming the liver and treating headaches, dizziness, tetanus, and epilepsy [11], as well as exerting novel pain relief and inflammatory-mediating activities [12]. Nevertheless, compared with its use as herb, its potential applications in materials science have been overlooked. Detailed investigations have revealed that G. elata consists of gastrodin and other heteroatom-containing compounds. Considering the enriched heteroatoms, it is reasonable to question whether such natural product can be used as a convenient and renewable source to functionalize carbon. On the basis of its high worldwide abundance [13], in this article, G. elata, a cheap and safe natural product, is adopted as a source of both carbon and nitrogen to synthesize N-doped carbons. Herein, for the first time, we report a one-stone-two-birds strategy for synthesizing N-doped carbon materials. The solid product (S1-AZ-800) exhibits excellent electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) via a dominant four-electron oxygen reduction pathway in a 0.1M KOH aqueous solution. Its onset potential (−0.06V) has a positive shift compared with many previous reports on N-doped carbon materials. Besides superior activity and methanol, and CO tolerance to a commercial Pt/C catalyst, S1-AZ-800 also displays excellent stability, i.e. no obvious decrease in current was observed after 15,000 continuous cycles between −0.7 and 0.06V in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH. Additionally, the supernatant sample (S2) is successfully demonstrated to behave as fluorescent decorative material combining with Chinese traditional paper-cut art and displaying mysterious and stimulating stage effects. Such high-efficiency methodology and excellent properties provide a new route to meet the rigorous requirements of commercial applications and environmental protection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the N-doped carbon materials
ce:section.ce:para: All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received. Natural G. elata was purchased from a drug store and washed by water for further use. The water used throughout all the experiments was deionized water.

In a typical synthesis of N-doped carbon materials, 3g of G. elata was put into a 50mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave containing 30mL deionized water, which was heated at 180°C for 24h, and then cooled down to ambient temperature naturally. The solid brownish product was washed by deionized water several times and then dried at room temperature for following ZnCl2 activation. After mixing the brownish solid product with ZnCl2 at a mass ratio of 1:3, the slurry was dried at 80°C, heated to 800°C with a rate of 10°C/min, and maintained at that temperature for 2h under N2 flow. The resultant intermediate was rinsed with 2molL−1 HCl and repeatedly washed with deionized water to remove any metal residue. The final activated carbons were dried under vacuum at 90°C for 2 days and named as S1-AZ-800. As a comparison, the sample without ZnCl2 activation was also prepared and marked as S1-BZ-800. The supernatant (S2) was kept as it is for further fluorescence characterization and application.

The glassy carbon working electrode (GCE) and rotating-disk glassy carbon electrode (RD-GCE) with diameters of 3mm and 4mm, respectively, were polished with a 0.05 and 0.3μm alpha alumina powder (CHI Instruments Inc.) suspensions on a micro-cloth, and subsequently rinsed with ultrapure water to a mirror finish. Then, the electrodes were sonicated in ultrapure water for 20min to remove any bound particles, rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure water and dried under a gentle air stream. To prepare the working electrode, homogeneous catalyst inks are prepared by ultrasonically dispersing the catalysts (ca. 1mg) in 190μL deionized water and 10μL 5wt% Nafion® solution (Dupont) for 30min at first. Thereafter, 5 and 10μL of the resulting suspension were dropped onto the mirror-polished GCE surface for 0.071cm2 and 0.126cm2, respectively. At last, the as-prepared electrodes were dried at room temperature, and then dried in vacuum overnight.

XRD patterns were recorded on a Rigaku MiniFlex II diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (1.406Å). SEM, TEM, and HRTEM images were taken on a Hitachi S4800-F high-resolution SEM and an H-7600 TEM (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. XPS measurements were performed on a VG Microtech ESCA 2000 using a monochromic Al X-ray source. Raman spectroscopy was carried out on a Renishaw inVia unit using the Ar ion laser with an excitation wavelength of 514.5nm. The nitrogen adsorption/desorption data were recorded at the liquid nitrogen temperature (77K) using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020M apparatus. Before the measurements, the samples were evacuated and degassed for 3h at 350°C. The specific surface areas were calculated by employing the BET method. FTIR was obtained on a FTS NEXUS spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet Corporation, USA) in the range of 400–4000cm−1. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd.). The PL spectra were collected on a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer.

All electrochemical experiments were performed at 25±1°C. ORR measurements were conducted on a computer-controlled CHI 660D electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode cell equipped with gas flow systems at 25°C. GCE or RD-GCE made in the above mentioned procedure was the working electrode and a piece of Pt foil (1cm2) served as the counter electrode. The Hg/HgO reference electrode (0.098V relative to reversible hydrogen electrode, RHE) was placed in a separate chamber, which was located near the working electrode chamber through a Luggin capillary tube. All electrochemical measurements were performed in 0.1M KOH solution. CV curves were collected by applying a linear potential scan at a sweep rate of 50mVs−1 between −0.7 and 0.06V after purging O2 or N2 gas for 30min. RDE voltammogram measurements were recorded at different rotating speeds from 400 to 2025rpm, and long term durability test was conducted with O2 continuous flow in electrolyte solution. The ORR current was obtained by subtracting the current measured in N2-saturated electrolyte from the current measured in O2-saturated electrolyte. The K–L plots were drawn by linear fitting of the reciprocal rotating speed versus reciprocal current density collected at −0.30V, −0.35V, −0.40V, −0.45V, −0.50V, −0.55V, −0.60V, −0.65V, −0.70V, and −0.75V. The slopes of their linear fitting were used to calculate the electron transfer number (n) on the basis of the K–L equation:
(1)

j

+

j

1

(2)
B = 0.2 n F
(
D
O
2
)
( 2 / 3 )
V
( − 1 / 6 )
C
O
2
where j is the experimentally measured current density, j
L is the diffusion-limiting current density, j
K is the kinetic current density, ω is the angular velocity, F is the Faraday constant (F=96,485Cmol−1),
C
O
2
is the bulk concentration of O2 (1.2×10−6 molcm−3),
D
O
2
is the diffusion coefficient of O2 in 0.1M KOH (1.9×10−5 cm2 s−1) and V is the kinematic viscosity (0.01cm2 s−1) of the electrolyte. The constant 0.2 is adopted when the rotation speed is expressed in rpm.

The cathode possible crossover and poison effects test in the presence of fuel molecules (e.g., methanol) and CO were performed by CVs and chronoamperometry, respectively. The stability of the catalyst was evaluated by the CVs in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH for 15,000 cycles. Fresh electrolyte solution was used for each electrochemical measurement to ensure reproducible results. All the potentials reported herein were versus the Hg/HgO electrode.

We cut an interesting Chinese traditional picture on commercially available filter paper and soak it in S2 followed by taking it out and drying it naturally. Then we took its photos under daylight and 356nm UV light, respectively.

Representative scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of S1-AZ-800 are presented in Figure 1A and B. A three-dimensional interconnected sponge consisting of fused microspheres with an average size of 4–5μm can be observed. As shown in Figure 1C, spherical particles are intercalated through disordered, network-like interstitial pores. Many nanopores are observed in the HRTEM image (Figure 1D). In the control experiment, although the product shows similar fused spherical morphologies (Figure S1), the sample without ZnCl2 activation (S1-BZ-800) reveals relatively smooth surface, which reflects its poor porosity. The corresponding X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of S1-AZ-800 is shown in Figure 1E. Two characteristic peaks are located at 2θ–26° and 44°, corresponding to the (002) and (101) diffraction planes of hexagonal graphite (JCPDS Card no. 41-1487), respectively [14]. These suggest the presence of graphitic carbon [15]. As well known, the carbon graphitization in the catalysts can enhance the electronic conductivity and corrosion resistance during electrocatalysis [16]. The S1-AZ-800 has a considerably large Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of ca. 1721m2 g−1, as determined by nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms (Figure 1F). This value exceeds those of many representative commercial high surface area materials, such as mesoporous silica SBA-15 (750m2 g−1), and reached the level of activated carbon YP17 (1700m2 g−1) [17]. In particular, an initial rise below the relative pressure of about 0.1 can be accounted for monolayer adsorption on microporous carbon layers, which is similar to the reported data for phenolic resin carbons and hollow core porous shell carbon spheres [18]. The V
a
–t plot further confirmed the presence of micropores in S1-AZ-800 (Figure S2a). The formation of the micropores might arise from the release of non-carbon components during the carbonization process, similar to the reported case [19]. In addition, a steep adsorption occurs around P/P 0=0.9–1.0, which is assigned to the macropores generated by ionothermal treatment. Such high specific surface area and porous structure are greatly favorable for applications that require rapid mass transport of both chemical and ionic species [20]. Comparatively, S1-BZ-800 presents a type IV sorption isotherm with a relatively low BET surface area of 398.5m2 g−1 (Figure S2b). Their pore size distributions and textural characteristics are displayed in Figure S2c, d and Table 1.

Figure 2A shows Raman spectrum of S1-AZ-800, which presents two featured Raman shifts of carbon. The G-band at 1588cm−1 is associated with the E 2g mode of graphitic carbon, while the D-band located at 1357cm−1 corresponds to the defect-induced mode, resulting from the possible amorphous carbon with high content of N and O heteroatoms in S1-AZ-800 [21]. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR) was used to identify the functional groups originating from N and O heteroatoms in S1-AZ-800. As shown in Figure 2B, the peaks at about 3434 and 1052cm−1 can be ascribed to the characteristic absorption bands of the −OH stretching vibration mode, while the peaks at 1677 and 1558cm−1 can be attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO−, respectively [22]. The characteristic absorption band of C−OH stretching at 1282cm−1 is also observed [23]. Additionally, the peak at ∼1499cm−1 is due to C–N stretching vibration and the peaks at ca. 2300cm−1and 1380cm−1 reveal the existence of the N-related bonds [24]. All of the above FTIR results indicate that the surfaces of S1-AZ-800 are rich in N-, O-containing groups.

To further determine the chemical composition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. As shown in Figure 2C, three typical peaks corresponding to the binding energies of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s are observed in the wide XPS survey scan, which confirms a certain doping level of N and O for S1-AZ-800. Quantitative analysis elucidates the corresponding atom contents: C—88.83%, N—3.07%, and O—8.11%. The N content of S1-AZ-800 is higher than those of S1-BZ-800 and other N-doped carbon materials [25]. Compared with the conventional in-situ and post-synthesized modifications (e.g. NH3 treatment of carbon materials at high temperature) [26], our work provides a green and cost-effective route to achieve high N content and distinctive microstructure. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Figure 2D) distinguished several types of carbon species. Graphitic carbon (CåC, 284.5eV) is the major form. Other peaks are due to secondary carbon (C*3C3OR, C3N, at around 286.1eV) and carbon bonded to oxygen or nitrogen (CåN, CåO, COOH, at around 288.8eV). The successful incorporation of N in the product was also confirmed by XPS analysis and displayed in Figure 2E. The N 1s signals can be deconvoluted into four different types of nitrogen species: pyridinic (398.5eV), pyrrolic (400.3eV), predominant quaternary (401.3eV), and oxidized nitrogen (405.2eV) (see Figure 2E and Table S1) [27]. The more the pyridinic nitrogen, the more the favorable conditions for ORR [28]. The possible structure of S1-AZ-800 is shown in Figure 2F.

Fuel cells can directly convert chemical energy into electric energy with high conversion efficiency, high power density, quiet operation, and no pollution, thus offering a great opportunity to obtain cleaner and more sustainable energy in the 21st century. Among many factors affecting the chemical–electrical energy conversion, ORR on cathode is the pivot in a fuel cell because this reaction is very slow in nature. Catalysts must be used to facilitate the four-electron pathway to boost the efficiency of fuel cells. Therefore, electrocatalysts for ORR are currently at the forefront of materials research. Up to now, the most efficient catalysts for ORR have still been platinum (Pt)-based materials [29]. However, the high costs, scarcity of precious metals, inferior durability, and susceptibility to methanol crossover, time-dependent drift [30], and CO poisoning [31], used for ORR catalysts, hamper the large-scale commercialization of these technologies [32]. In this regard, N-doped carbon materials are of particular interest owing to their catalytic activity towards the ORR, as confirmed by both experimental studies [33] and quantum-mechanical calculations [34].

The abundance and the electrochemical availability of the N species in S1-AZ-800 described here suggest that it could serve as an ORR electrocatalyst. In order to explore this possibility, cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were carried out through the conventional three-electrode method in O2 or N2 saturated 0.1M KOH aqueous solutions (Figure 3A (1)). All the potentials reported herein were versus the Hg/HgO electrode. Despite the capacitive background, a characteristic ORR peak appeared at ca. −0.12V in the O2 saturated solution, while featureless character was observed in the O2-absent medium. This value is positively shifted, about 200mV and 240mV, compared to S1-BZ-800 (Figure 3A (2), –0.32V) and the bare glassy carbon electrode (Figure 3A (3), −0.36V), indicating the effective electrochemical reduction of oxygen initiated on S1-AZ-800. As discussed above, the specific surface area was highly increased after ZnCl2 activation, which was favorable for rapid mass transport of both chemical and ionic species. Besides this, the amount of N species and especially that of pyridinic-N were also greatly enhanced, which can facilitate O2 adsorption and subsequently, the ORR process by reducing O2 to two H2O [35]. Its enhanced ORR performance could be directly ascribed to the efficient introduction of N in 3D interconnected porous structure resulting from ZnCl2 activation.

To further investigate the ORR catalytic activity, S1-AZ-800, in comparison with the directly carbonized product (S1-BZ-800) and commercial 20wt% Pt/C (JM), was tested using an RDE in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution. The ORR onset potentials of each sample were acquired from RDE linear sweep at 1600rpm (Figure S3 and Table S2). Although it is slightly lower than that of Pt/C electrode just like other N-containing carbon materials for ORR [36], the starting potential of ORR on S1-AZ-800 (−0.06V) also has a positive shift compared with S1-BZ-800 and many previous reports on N-doped carbon materials [37], indicating that the ORR process occurred much easily on S1-AZ-800. On the other hand, similar to that of Pt/C catalyst, diffusion limited current on S1-AZ-800 is reached quickly before −0.3V, showing the efficient reactant transfer through the 3D interconnected porous structure. Linear sweep voltammograms (LSVs) from 400 to 2025rpm and Koutecky–Levich (K–L) plots with a good linear relationship for the S1-AZ-800 electrode are shown in Figure 3B and the inset. The mean transferred electron number (n) per O2 molecule involved in ORR is determined from the K–L equation (Eqs. (1) and (2)) to be approximately 4.1, suggesting that the ORR proceeds on S1-AZ-800 via a four-electron pathway, as in the case of Pt/C catalyst. Based on the BET and XPS characterizations, the large performance difference between S1-AZ-800 and S1-BZ-800 may be ascribed to the quite different BET surface areas and the amount and type of N species [35,38]. These results indicate that the ZnCl2 activation had a great effect on the heteroatom doping nature, as well as on their configuration characteristic of 3D interconnected porous structure and application as a promising metal-free catalyst for the ORR in an alkaline solution.

For an ORR electrocatalyst of fuel cells, a strong resistance to fuel crossover is highly pursued because small-molecule organic fuels, such as methanol, could cross over through the polymer electrolyte membrane from anode to cathode and seriously affect the whole cell performance [39]. Figure 3C and D depicts the CVs for O2 reduction in the presence of methanol on S1-AZ-800 in comparison with a 20% Pt/C catalyst. A well-defined cathodic peak is clearly seen in the O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution without showing obvious change after addition of methanol (10vol%) into the electrolyte (Figure 3C), suggesting the remarkable tolerance of S1-AZ-800 to possible crossover effects. For the commercial Pt/C electrode, however, the cathodic peak for oxygen reduction disappeared in the O2-saturated electrolyte with 10vol% methanol, while a reversed anodic current was observed on Pt/C electrode associated with methanol oxidation [40] (Figure 3D). Such high selectivity of the S1-AZ-800 electrode toward the ORR and remarkably good tolerance to crossover effect can be attributed to the much lower ORR potential than that required for electro-oxidation of the fuel molecules [41]. To examine the effect of CO on the electrocatalytic activity of S1-AZ-800, 10% (v/v) CO in air was introduced into the electrolyte. The S1-AZ-800 electrode was insensitive to CO (Figure 3E), whereas the Pt/C electrode was rapidly poisoned under the same conditions. The durability of S1-AZ-800 and Pt/C was also assessed through continuous potential cycling. As can be seen in Figure 3F, no obvious decrease in current was observed after 15,000 continuous cycles between −0.7 and 0.06V in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH. In contrast, the Pt/C electrode exhibited a gradual decrease, with a current loss of approximately 31.4% measured after 15,000 cycles, which confirms a much better stability of ORR on S1-AZ-800 than that on commercial Pt/C in alkaline environment. The superior crossover resistance and CO tolerance could be attributed to the incorporation of N atoms into the porous carbon framework, which favors the reactivity of the neighborly linked carbon atoms via alteration of the electronic structure as in the case of N-doped carbon nanotubes [42], high specific surface area, and interconnected framework for efficient diffusion of O2 molecules.

Here, what we should point out is why we set 800°C as the ionothermal temperature. In fact, we prepared a series of samples at the ionothermal temperatures of 600°C, 700°C, and 900°C, and they were named as S1-AZ-600, S1-AZ-700, and S1-AZ-900, respectively. The electrochemical experiments with them deposited on glassy carbon electrodes (in the same way as described in the experimental section) were conducted in both N2-saturated (control) and O2-saturated aqueous solutions of 0.1M KOH. The CVs shown in Figure S4 clearly demonstrate their inferior electrocatalytic activities for ORR to that of S1-AZ-800. LSVs from 400 to 2025rpm, K–L plots, and the results for these three are shown in Figure S5 and Table S2. The mean transferred electron numbers per O2 molecule involved in ORR are determined from the K–L equation to be approximately 3.0 for S1-AZ-600, 3.1 for S1-AZ-700, and 4.0 for S1-AZ-900. This suggests the first formation of peroxide and then partial reduction to water during the oxygen-reduction process on S1-AZ-600 and S1-AZ-700, while the ORR proceeds on S1-AZ-900 via a four-electron pathway similar to that on S1-AZ-800. Based on these, we just take S1-AZ-800 as an example to explicitly report our results. This is well accordant with the literature describing the notable effect of temperature on the performance of materials [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the supernatant product S2
ce:section.ce:para: For hydrothermal synthesis, solid product and supernatant are generated at the same time. To the best of our knowledge, till now, either the solid product or the supernatant has been discarded. This deviates far from the well-accepted concept that all the issues should be considered to gain an advantage. With this in mind, we also studied the supernatant product, S2. Figure 4A shows its UV−vis absorption spectrum. An absorption band at ca. 280nm may be attributed to n–π ⁎ transitions of CåO and is blue-shifted by ca. 40nm with respect to that of N-free carbon dots [44]. Under irradiation by a 365nm lamp, the S2 emits intense blue luminescence (Figure 4A inset). It has been reported that isolated sp2-hybridized clusters within the carbon−oxygen matrix could yield band gaps consistent with blue emission due to the localization of electron−hole pairs [45]. It is also suggested that surface functionality (O, N-rich groups) makes an important contribution to the observed blue shift in the PL emission from S2 [46]. The relatively strong electron affinity of N atoms in S2 could also contribute to the photoluminescence (PL) blue shift. Indeed, previous experimental observations and quantum-mechanical calculations have proved the strong electron-withdrawing ability of the N atoms within the conjugated C plane [47]. Like other carbon dots, S2 shows clear excitation-dependent PL emission wavelength and intensity (Figure 4B) [48]. These results indicate that the as-prepared S2 possesses unique optoelectronic properties due to the N-doping-induced modulation of the chemical and electronic characteristics of S2. It is worth mentioning that the dispersion can be very stable for several months.

With the improvement of human living level, the demand for theatrical materials is rapidly increasing. Herein, we combine the fluorescence performance together with Chinese folk paper-cut art to expand the potential application of S2. As shown in Figure 4C, the picture on a commercially available filter paper after adsorbing S2 appears pretty blue under UV light, accordant with that in Figure 4A inset and quite different from that under daylight (Figure 4D). We can imagine that such materials would become the new-generation decorative materials and display fascinating stage effects.

In summary, we reported a one-stone-two-birds strategy for synthesizing N-doped carbon materials via hydrothermal treatment of G. elata instead of the commonly used techniques for N-doping of carbon materials that requires high energy consumption, expensive hardware, multistep processes, and expensive, toxic or flammable chemicals. To the best of our knowledge, we focused on both the solid product and the supernatant for the first time and used the best advantages of the two. The former treated by further ionothermal pyrolysis exhibits an impressive ORR catalytic activity (e.g. an electron-exchange number of four) that was comparable to many previous reports on N-doped carbons. The long-term stability, tolerance to crossover, and anti-poison ability are also better than for standard Pt/C used for oxygen reduction in an alkaline electrolyte. The ease in production and low-cost suggests considerable room for cost-effective preparation of metal-free efficient carbon-based catalysts for oxygen reduction. The supernatant exhibits strong blue fluorescence under UV radiation similar to many documented carbon quantum dots. Combined with Chinese folk paper-cut art, it shows promising potential as new-generation decorative materials and displays striking stage effects. The rational combination of N-containing biomolecules (e.g. of different size, configuration, and reactivity) may offer numerous potentialities in creating interesting functional materials for diverse applications. Work focused in these directions is currently going on.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 21071047), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-11-0944), the Excellent Youth Foundation of He'nan Scientific Committee (124100510004), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (2011HASTIT010). D. G. and Y. B. thank MANA-NIMS for support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2013.06.005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Journal of Traditional Chinese Medical Literature
Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
Journal of the American Chemical Society
Journal of the American Chemical Society
Journal of the American Chemical Society
Journal of the American Chemical Society
Journal of the American Chemical Society

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hao Fan is a postgraduate student in Prof. Shuyan Gao’s research group at Henan Normal University. His current research interests is the development of (i) non-precious metal and (ii) nitrogen-doped carbon and their composites with controlled nanostructures for fuel cell and battery applications.

Yanli Chen is a postgraduate student in Prof. Shuyan Gao’s research group at Henan Normal University. Her research focuses on fluorescent carbon dots and carbon-related materials for sensing and sustainable energy applications.

Shuyan Gao received her Ph.D. from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and won Liu YongLing Special Award in 2006. From 2007 to 2009, she worked as a JSPS research fellow at the National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology. Afterwards, she was a postdoctoral researcher at Hokkaido University. Now, she is a full professor at Henan Normal University. Her research includes green synthesis of nitrogen-rich carbon-based materials and their composites with controllable dimensions and the applications in energy conversion and storage, sensors, and optoelectronics.

Dr. Liang Li was born in Anhui province, China, in 1979. He received his Ph.D. degree from the Institute of Solid State Physics (ISSP), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) and won the Excellent President Scholarship in 2006. From 2007–2012, he worked in National University of Singapore (NUS), Singapore, National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), Japan, National Institute of Materials Science (NIMS), Japan, and the University of Western Ontario (UWO), Canada. Since August 2012, he is a full professor in School of Physical Science and Technology, Soochow University in China. His research group focuses mainly on the controlled synthesis, novel physical properties and optoelectronic devices of low-dimensional nanomaterials, particularly works on nanostructures for solar cells. He has published over 90 refereed publications, which have generated over 2000 citations. The H-index of his publications is 27.

Prof. Yoshio Bando received his Ph.D. degree from Osaka University in 1975 and joined the National Institute for Research in Inorganic Materials (at present NIMS) in the same year. From 1979 to 1981 he worked as a visiting researcher at Arizona State University. Currently, he is a Chief Operating Officer (COO) of the International Center for Materials Nanoarchitectonics (MANA) and a Fellow within the National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS). He is also a visiting Professor at both Waseda University and the University of Tokyo. His current research concentrates on the synthesis and properties of various inorganic nanostructures and their TEM characterizations. He has published more than 500 papers in international journals and the total number of citations of his papers is over 17,000. The H-index of his publications is 69. He also holds more than 150 patents. He received the 16th Tsukuba Prize in 2005 for his studies on novel inorganic nanotubes and nanothermometers and the 2011 Thomson Reuters Research Front Award for his investigations on 1D-nanostructures.

Prof. Dmitri Golberg obtained his Ph.D. degree from the Central Research Institute for Ferrous Metallurgy, Moscow, Russia, in 1990, and joined the National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS), Tsukuba, Japan, five years later. To date, he has authored and co-authored more than 450 original papers and nearly 100 patents. His papers have been cited over 12,000 times. The H-index of his publications is 58. In 2005 he was awarded the 16th Tsukuba Prize for his studies on inorganic nanotubes using electron microscopy, also in 2012 he became a recipient of the 3rd Thomson Reuters Research Front Award for his studies on 1D-nanomaterials. He is currently a Unit Director and a Principal Investigator within the WPI International Center for Materials Nanoarchitectonics (MANA) of NIMS, and a Professor of the University of Tsukuba. His current research focuses on nanoscale material electrical and mechanical property studies in a transmission electron microscope.
